original_abstract: Organic anion transporting polypeptide 1B1 (OATP1B1) is a critical multispeciﬁc hepatic transporter responsible for the uptake of a wide range of endogenous compounds and clinical drugs, including bile salts, bilirubin, and statins. Despite its central role in drug pharmacokinetics and drug–drug interactions (DDIs), the structural basis of its broad substrate recognition and the mechanics of its transport cycle have remained poorly understood. In this study, we present cryo-EM structures of human OATP1B1 in both outward- and inward-open states, captured in its apo form and in complex with representative ligands: the endogenous metabolites bilirubin and estrone-3-sulfate, the antiviral drug simeprevir, and the fluorescent probe 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein. These structures identify a central translocation path characterized by an orthosteric binding hub of conserved aromatic residues, supplemented by distinct major and minor pockets that accommodate diverse chemical scaffolds. Our findings reveal that OATP1B1 facilitates transport through a rocker-switch mechanism involving significant conformational rearrangements of the transmembrane domains. Integration of these structural data with molecular dynamics simulations and mutagenesis functional assays comprehensively elucidates the molecular logic behind OATP1B1 polyspecificity. This work provides a definitive structural framework for understanding the OATP family and offers a template for predicting DDIs and optimizing drug delivery to the liver.
humanized_abstract: Organic anion transporting polypeptide 1B1 (OATP1B1) is a multispecific hepatic transporter that takes up a large number of endogenous substrates and clinical drugs including bile salts, bilirubin and statins. Despite its key role in drug pharmacokinetics and drug–drug interactions (DDIs), the structural reason for its polyspecificity and how it functions during transport is poorly understood. Here, we report cryo-EM structures of human OATP1B1 in its outward- and inward-open conformations in both its apo form as well as with its representative ligands such as the endogenous compounds bilirubin and estrone-3-sulfate as well as the antiviral drug simeprevir and the fluorescent dye 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein. Together, these show that the functional translocation path contains a conserved orthosteric aromatic binding hub along with additional substrate-binding major and minor pockets. Substrate translocation occurs through a rocker-switch mechanism, which involves substantial changes in the TMDs (transmembrane domains). Through the integration of these structures with a combination of molecular dynamics simulations and point mutants, the structural basis for the polyspecificity of OATP1B1 is fully explained and serves as a paradigm for understanding the structure and function of members of the OATP family as well as in the prediction of DDI and improved drug delivery to the liver.

Together, these show that the functional translocation path contains a conserved orthosteric aromatic binding hub along with additional substrate-binding major and minor pockets.
